metadata.title: He Had 5 Minutes to Decide
metadata.global_visual_style: Historical archival photo style, Kodachrome 60s–80s film grain, cinematic noir lighting, high-contrast shadows, desaturated Cold War palette with occasional bursts of amber and red console glow. 9:16 vertical framing. Fast cuts — visual change every 3 seconds minimum.
metadata.bg_music_note: No background music. Intentional — maximizes Shorts revenue pool allocation. All audio is narration and SFX only.

timeline.narration: On September 26th, 1983, one man had 5 minutes to decide if World War III had started.
timeline.visual_description: Black screen. Large white sans-serif text snaps in: 'HE HAD 5 MINUTES.' Cold, stark, no other visual elements. 9:16 vertical frame.

timeline.narration: Just past midnight. In a fortified Soviet bunker sixty-two miles south of Moscow, Stanislav Petrov sits alone at an early-warning console.
timeline.visual_description: Medium shot of a lone Soviet officer in profile, face partially lit by green monitor glow in a darkened underground bunker. Kodachrome 80s grain, deep shadow, cinematic noir.
timeline.sfx_prompt: faint ventilation hum, quiet clock ticking

timeline.narration: He wasn't even supposed to be there. He was covering for an absent officer.
timeline.visual_description: Over-the-shoulder shot looking at a glowing early-warning display board, an empty chair to the left suggesting an absent colleague. Cold blue and amber lighting, Kodachrome grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: quiet ambient hum, footstep echo in the distance

timeline.narration: Then the alarm hits. The screen flashes a single word in red: LAUNCH.
timeline.visual_description: Extreme close-up of a Soviet warning panel — the ЗАПУСК label blazes red, backlit, filling the frame. Cinematic noir lighting, heavy Kodachrome grain, lens slightly out of focus for dread effect.
timeline.sfx_prompt: sharp alarm blare, red console light flickering on

timeline.narration: The system says five American intercontinental ballistic missiles are inbound. Headed for the Soviet Union.
timeline.visual_description: Early-warning display screen showing five glowing trajectory lines arcing from North America toward the USSR. Cold blue monitor glow in total darkness. Kodachrome 80s grain.

timeline.narration: Protocol is clear: report it up the chain immediately. The Soviet nuclear arsenal goes on alert.
timeline.visual_description: Extreme close-up of Petrov's hand hovering over a red telephone receiver, not yet lifting it. Console glow illuminates the hand from below. Soviet military insignia on the wrist cuff. Cinematic noir.
timeline.sfx_prompt: alarm blaring, phone ready to ring, tense silence beneath

timeline.narration: But something doesn't feel right. A real American first strike would launch hundreds of missiles. Not five.
timeline.visual_description: Petrov's face reflected in the console screen, expression of focused doubt, green light washing over his features. Slow push-in toward his eyes. Kodachrome 80s film grain, deep blacks.

timeline.narration: He also knows the satellite system is new. Untested under these exact conditions.
timeline.visual_description: Schematic diagram of the Soviet Oko early-warning satellite system, orbit lines over a Cold War-era globe. Aged technical document style, blueprint tones, Kodachrome grain.

timeline.narration: He picks up the phone. He tells his superiors it's a false alarm. He has no proof. Just instinct.
timeline.visual_description: Petrov's hand lifts the red receiver. His lips moving. Expression steady, voice calm. Side-lit by console amber glow, deep shadow behind. Kodachrome 80s grain, cinematic noir.
timeline.sfx_prompt: phone receiver lifting, quiet voice, alarm continuing in background

timeline.narration: For twenty-three minutes, the alarm keeps screaming. Every second is a second he could be wrong.
timeline.visual_description: Analog clock face, second hand sweeping, backlit red. Rapid cuts between the clock and the blaring alarm panel. Heavy Kodachrome grain, extreme close-up, dread lighting.
timeline.sfx_prompt: clock ticking loud, alarm pulsing, silence underneath threatening

timeline.narration: Then ground radar confirms it. No missiles. A satellite malfunction — sunlight reflecting off cloud tops had fooled the sensor.
timeline.visual_description: Ground radar display showing clear, empty skies — no inbound tracks. A Soviet technician leaning back, expression of exhaled relief. Amber overhead light, Kodachrome 80s grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: alarm cutting off abruptly, sudden silence, exhale

timeline.narration: Petrov was right. He never received a commendation. He was quietly reprimanded for failing to fill in his logbook correctly that night.
timeline.visual_description: Close-up of a logbook, a page conspicuously blank where entries should be. Red administrative stamp on the side. Cold institutional lighting, Kodachrome grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: pen set down on desk, quiet bureaucratic silence

timeline.narration: He died in 2017. Most of the world had no idea he existed. The full story is on the channel.
timeline.visual_description: Channel-branded card — the Stanislav Petrov long-form documentary thumbnail with bold text overlay: 'FULL STORY: 21 MINUTES'. Chronicles of Eras channel name. Dark background, clean composition.
LOOP SETUP — identical to opening frame: Black screen. White text snaps in: 'HE HAD 5 MINUTES.' Holds for 5 seconds. Seamless replay trigger.
